justification: Both domain rules present with testable_assertions. All three architecture decisions present with implementation_notes (exceeds minimum). acceptance_criteria preserves ORDER_STATE_INVALID verbatim. task_context is specific and actionable. No invented information. However, terminology_alignment does not address the key rubric requirement: reconciling Bird's use of 'SHIPPED' / 'order status' with MJ's 'PATCH /orders/{id}/state' / 'state' — confirming these refer to the same concept. Instead it defines generic terms ('state invariant', 'ORDER_STATE_INVALID') that don't bridge the Bird/MJ language gap.

observations.text: Both invariant rules (state transition + refund precondition) present with clear, specific testable_assertions
Architecture guidance covers all three decisions (PATCH endpoint, OrderStateMachine, RefundService) with substantive implementation_notes
acceptance_criteria preserves ORDER_STATE_INVALID error code verbatim and uses precise Given/When/Then format
task_context is a single paragraph that accurately summarizes what Shaq is building and why
No invented information — all details traceable to source summaries

terminology_alignment misses the core requirement: Bird says 'SHIPPED' and 'order status' while MJ says 'PATCH /orders/{id}/state' and 'state' — Magic should explicitly confirm these refer to the same concept and clarify that 'state' is the technical term for Bird's 'order status'
terminology_alignment entries ('state invariant' and 'ORDER_STATE_INVALID') are generic glossary definitions rather than reconciliations of actual language differences between Bird and MJ

trials.justification: Both domain rules present with specific testable_assertions. All three architecture decisions present (PATCH endpoint, OrderStateMachine, RefundService) with implementation_notes — exceeds the two required. acceptance_criteria preserves ORDER_STATE_INVALID verbatim in the first criterion. terminology_alignment is present but addresses domain-to-technical mapping rather than directly reconciling Bird's 'order status' language with MJ's 'state' terminology — this is adequate but not ideal. task_context is one specific, actionable paragraph. No invented information detected.

observations.text: Three architecture decisions instead of two — includes OrderStateMachine as a separate decision with good implementation notes about it being a pure domain object
acceptance_criteria uses precise Given/When/Then format and preserves ORDER_STATE_INVALID error code verbatim
Refund acceptance_criteria covers both happy path (DELIVERED) and rejection path (non-DELIVERED), which is thorough
Low confidence areas and assumptions are well-calibrated — correctly flags that the full state graph is underspecified

terminology_alignment does not directly address the Bird/MJ language gap: Bird says 'SHIPPED' (domain status) and MJ says 'PATCH /orders/{id}/state' (technical endpoint) — Magic should have explicitly noted that Bird's 'order status' and MJ's 'state' refer to the same concept
Third acceptance criterion (reject refund on non-delivered) says 'appropriate error' rather than specifying a concrete error code, losing some precision compared to the first criterion

trials.justification: Both domain rules present with testable_assertions. All three architecture decisions present with implementation_notes (exceeds requirement). acceptance_criteria preserves ORDER_STATE_INVALID verbatim. task_context is specific and actionable. No invented information. However, terminology_alignment does NOT address the core Bird/MJ language difference the rubric requires: Bird says 'SHIPPED' (order status) while MJ says 'PATCH /orders/{id}/state' (technical endpoint) — Magic should explicitly reconcile that Bird's 'order status' maps to MJ's 'state' field. Instead, the terminology_alignment entries cover 'order state invariant' and 'revert-to-pending', which are useful but miss the specific reconciliation called out in the rubric.

observations.text: Both invariant rules (state transition prohibition and refund precondition) present with concrete, testable assertions including HTTP status codes and error codes
Architecture guidance covers all three decisions (PATCH endpoint, OrderStateMachine, RefundService) with clear implementation notes
acceptance_criteria preserves ORDER_STATE_INVALID error code verbatim in Given/When/Then format
task_context is a single paragraph that clearly summarizes what Shaq is building and why, referencing both Bird and MJ contributions
No invented information — all details trace back to the described source summaries

terminology_alignment misses the specific reconciliation the rubric requires: Bird uses 'SHIPPED' and 'order status' while MJ uses 'state' in 'PATCH /orders/{id}/state' — Magic should confirm these refer to the same concept and clarify that 'state' is the technical term for Bird's 'order status'
Extra acceptance criterion for the happy path (refund on DELIVERED order) adds value without inventing information
Low confidence area correctly flags that only one illegal transition is specified, which is a genuine gap Shaq would need to address
